reviews.text: The book every junior engineer should read. Pragmatic, opinionated, full of small lessons about software craftsmanship. Quick read, high practicality.
Updated edition still feels relevant. Short chapters make it easy to dip into. Light on deep theory, heavy on principles you can apply tomorrow.
The bible of distributed systems. Dense, deep, takes weeks to work through but every chapter rewards the time. Best treatment of consistency models I've read.
Massive book about how data systems actually work under the hood. Hard but worth it. The chapter on stream processing alone is gold.
Comprehensive systems design reference. Some sections are a slog but the depth is unmatched. Not for beginners.
Beautiful storytelling about how computers actually work, starting from binary and building up to a CPU. Reads like a novel. Surprisingly accessible.
Petzold turns computer fundamentals into narrative. Wonderful introduction to the field for someone who wants to understand the machine, not just program it.
Fowler's catalog of refactorings is the practical complement to clean code. Heavy on examples in JavaScript this edition. Very actionable.
Refactoring patterns are timeless. Use this book as a reference; the catalog format makes it easy to look up specific transformations.
Builds two interpreters from scratch — one tree-walking, one bytecode VM. Incredibly clear writing. Best book on language implementation for working engineers.
A masterclass in how to write a teaching book. Crafty incremental progression. By the end you've actually built a working language.
Knuth's magnum opus. Extremely deep, mathematically rigorous, and pretty challenging. Not practical for day-to-day work but essential for serious algorithm study.
Foundational but dense. The MIX assembly distracts from the core ideas in places. Still, depth that no other algorithms book matches.
Goes deep into how JavaScript actually works — closures, this, prototypes. The kind of book that makes you re-evaluate code you've been writing for years.
If you want to understand JavaScript at the language level, this is the book. Approachable but never superficial. Highly practical.
Easy to read, full of clear principles for naming and small functions. A bit dogmatic in places but a great starting point for code quality.
Overrated in some circles. The advice is fine for greenfield code but some of the recommendations (tiny functions, no comments ever) don't survive contact with real codebases.
